title: Etta Jones

infobox.value: October 16, 2001(2001-10-16) (aged 72)  [Mount Vernon](/wiki/Mount_Vernon,_New_York "Mount Vernon, New York"), New York, U.S.

(November 25, 1928 – October 16, 2001) was an American jazz singer.[[1]](#cite_note-AMG-1) Her best-known recordings are "Don't Go to Strangers" and "Save Your Love for Me". She worked with [Buddy Johnson](/wiki/Buddy_Johnson "Buddy Johnson"), [Oliver Nelson](/wiki/Oliver_Nelson "Oliver Nelson"), [Earl Hines](/wiki/Earl_Hines "Earl Hines"), [Barney Bigard](/wiki/Barney_Bigard "Barney Bigard"), [Gene Ammons](/wiki/Gene_Ammons "Gene Ammons"), [Kenny Burrell](/wiki/Kenny_Burrell "Kenny Burrell"), [Milt Jackson](/wiki/Milt_Jackson "Milt Jackson"), [Cedar Walton](/wiki/Cedar_Walton "Cedar Walton"), and [Houston Person](/wiki/Houston_Person "Houston Person").[[2]](#cite_note-Dead-2)

From Wikipedia, the free encyclopedia

American jazz singer (1928–2001)

Not to be confused with the founding member of the [Dandridge Sisters](/wiki/Dandridge_Sisters "Dandridge Sisters") nor with [Etta James](/wiki/Etta_James "Etta James").

| Etta Jones | |
| --- | --- |
| [Jones and Houston Person, 1980](/wiki/File:Etta_Jones_1980.jpg "Jones and Houston Person, 1980") Jones and [Houston Person](/wiki/Houston_Person "Houston Person"), 1980 | |
| Background information | |
| Born | (1928-11-25)November 25, 1928  [Aiken](/wiki/Aiken,_South_Carolina "Aiken, South Carolina"), South Carolina, U.S. |
| Died | October 16, 2001(2001-10-16) (aged 72)  [Mount Vernon](/wiki/Mount_Vernon,_New_York "Mount Vernon, New York"), New York, U.S. |
| Genres | * [Jazz](/wiki/Jazz "Jazz") * [pop](/wiki/Pop_music "Pop music") * [R&B](/wiki/Rhythm_and_blues "Rhythm and blues") |
| Occupations | * Singer * songwriter |
| Instrument | Vocals |

Musical artist

**Etta Jones** (November 25, 1928 – October 16, 2001) was an American jazz singer.[[1]](#cite_note-AMG-1) Her best-known recordings are "Don't Go to Strangers" and "Save Your Love for Me". She worked with [Buddy Johnson](/wiki/Buddy_Johnson "Buddy Johnson"), [Oliver Nelson](/wiki/Oliver_Nelson "Oliver Nelson"), [Earl Hines](/wiki/Earl_Hines "Earl Hines"), [Barney Bigard](/wiki/Barney_Bigard "Barney Bigard"), [Gene Ammons](/wiki/Gene_Ammons "Gene Ammons"), [Kenny Burrell](/wiki/Kenny_Burrell "Kenny Burrell"), [Milt Jackson](/wiki/Milt_Jackson "Milt Jackson"), [Cedar Walton](/wiki/Cedar_Walton "Cedar Walton"), and [Houston Person](/wiki/Houston_Person "Houston Person").[[2]](#cite_note-Dead-2)

## Biography

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Etta_Jones&action=edit&section=1 "Edit section: Biography")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/0/05/Etta_Jones1.jpg/250px-Etta_Jones1.jpg)](/wiki/File:Etta_Jones1.jpg)

Jones in Bryant Park, New York City, 1984

### Early life

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Etta_Jones&action=edit&section=2 "Edit section: Early life")]

Jones was born in [Aiken, South Carolina](/wiki/Aiken,_South_Carolina "Aiken, South Carolina"),[[1]](#cite_note-AMG-1) where her parents named her after another Etta Jones, a member of the [Dandridge Sisters](/wiki/Dandridge_Sisters "Dandridge Sisters").[[3]](#cite_note-:0-3) She was raised in [Harlem](/wiki/Harlem "Harlem"), New York, where she began performing as a teenager. After she impressed [Buddy Johnson](/wiki/Buddy_Johnson "Buddy Johnson") at an amateur night at the Apollo Theater in Harlem, he invited her to join his band as a vocalist while his sister, [Ella Johnson](/wiki/Ella_Johnson "Ella Johnson"), was on maternity leave.[[3]](#cite_note-:0-3) Still in her teens, she joined the band for a tour although she was not featured on record. Her first recordings—"Salty Papa Blues", "[Evil Gal Blues](/wiki/Evil_Man%27s_Blues "Evil Man's Blues")", "Blow Top Blues", and "Long, Long Journey"—were produced by [Leonard Feather](/wiki/Leonard_Feather "Leonard Feather") in 1944, placing her in the company of clarinetist [Barney Bigard](/wiki/Barney_Bigard "Barney Bigard") and tenor saxophonist [Georgie Auld](/wiki/Georgie_Auld "Georgie Auld").[[1]](#cite_note-AMG-1)

### Recording Career

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Etta_Jones&action=edit&section=3 "Edit section: Recording Career")]

In 1947, she recorded and released an early cover version of [Leon René](/wiki/Leon_Ren%C3%A9 "Leon René")'s "[I Sold My Heart to the Junkman](/wiki/I_Sold_My_Heart_to_the_Junkman "I Sold My Heart to the Junkman")" (previously released by the Basin Street Boys on Rene's [Exclusive](/wiki/Exclusive_Records "Exclusive Records") label) while at [RCA Victor Records](/wiki/RCA_Victor_Records "RCA Victor Records").[[4]](#cite_note-4) She performed with the [Earl Hines](/wiki/Earl_Hines "Earl Hines") sextet from 1949 to 1952.[[5]](#cite_note-5)

During the 1950s, Jones sought to obtain a recording contract, while working as a seamstress, elevator operator, and album stuffer. In 1956, she released the album *The Jones Girl...Etta...Sings, Sings, Sings.* She obtained a contract in 1960 with [Prestige Records](/wiki/Prestige_Records "Prestige Records") after impressing Prestige's preparatory director, [Esmond Edwards](/wiki/Esmond_Edwards "Esmond Edwards"), with her singing's warmth and phrasing via an unsolicited demo tape. Her first single, "Don't Go to Strangers," hit number five on the Rhythm and Blues chart, and 36 in pop charts, at a time when pop charts were dominated by white men. Over the next three years, she recorded ten albums for Prestige. Her favorite songwriter to cover was [Sammy Cahn](/wiki/Sammy_Cahn "Sammy Cahn"), and she also favored [Harold Arlen](/wiki/Harold_Arlen "Harold Arlen"), [George](/wiki/George_Gershwin "George Gershwin") and [Ira Gershwin](/wiki/Ira_Gershwin "Ira Gershwin"), and [Cole Porter](/wiki/Cole_Porter "Cole Porter").[[3]](#cite_note-:0-3)

### Partnership with Houston Person

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Etta_Jones&action=edit&section=4 "Edit section: Partnership with Houston Person")]

Following these recordings, on which Jones was featured with high-profile arrangers such as [Oliver Nelson](/wiki/Oliver_Nelson "Oliver Nelson") and jazz stars such as [Frank Wess](/wiki/Frank_Wess "Frank Wess"), [Roy Haynes](/wiki/Roy_Haynes "Roy Haynes"), and [Gene Ammons](/wiki/Gene_Ammons "Gene Ammons"), she had a musical partnership of more than 30 years with tenor saxophonist [Houston Person](/wiki/Houston_Person "Houston Person"), who received equal billing with her.[[6]](#cite_note-6) He also produced her albums and served as her manager after the pair met in one of [Johnny "Hammond" Smith](/wiki/Johnny_%22Hammond%22_Smith "Johnny \"Hammond\" Smith")'s bands.

Although Etta Jones is likely to be remembered above all for her recordings on Prestige, she had a very productive musical career in the last two decades of her life. She had a close professional relationship with Person (frequently, but mistakenly, identified as Jones's husband), and they performed together for decades. Starting in 1976, they began recording for [Muse](/wiki/Muse_Records "Muse Records"), which later changed its name to [HighNote](/wiki/HighNote_Records "HighNote Records"). Person became her manager, as well as her record producer and accompanist, in a partnership that lasted until her death in 2001.[[7]](#cite_note-:1-7) They performed up to 200 times a year until she had to stop due to her health. Jones had dealt with cancer for more than a decade until the time of her death and had a mastectomy and chemotherapy.[[3]](#cite_note-:0-3)

### Legacy and death

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Etta_Jones&action=edit&section=5 "Edit section: Legacy and death")]

Only one of her recordings—her debut album for [Prestige Records](/wiki/Prestige_Records "Prestige Records") (*[Don't Go to Strangers](/wiki/Don%27t_Go_to_Strangers "Don't Go to Strangers")*, 1960)—enjoyed commercial success with sales of more than 1 million copies. However, her remaining seven albums for Prestige, and beginning in 1976, her recordings for [Muse Records](/wiki/Muse_Records "Muse Records"), and for [HighNote Records](/wiki/HighNote_Records "HighNote Records") secured her a devoted following.[[1]](#cite_note-AMG-1) She had three [Grammy](/wiki/Grammy_Awards "Grammy Awards") nominations: for the *[Don't Go to Strangers](/wiki/Don%27t_Go_to_Strangers "Don't Go to Strangers")* album in 1960, the *[Save Your Love for Me](/wiki/Save_Your_Love_for_Me "Save Your Love for Me")* album in 1981, and *[My Buddy](/wiki/My_Buddy:_Etta_Jones_Sings_the_Songs_of_Buddy_Johnson "My Buddy: Etta Jones Sings the Songs of Buddy Johnson")*[[7]](#cite_note-:1-7) (dedicated to her first employer, [Buddy Johnson](/wiki/Buddy_Johnson "Buddy Johnson")) in 1998. In 2008, the album *Don't Go to Strangers* was inducted into the [Grammy Hall of Fame](/wiki/List_of_Grammy_Hall_of_Fame_Award_recipients_A-D "List of Grammy Hall of Fame Award recipients A-D").[[8]](#cite_note-8) In 1996, she recorded the jazz vocalist tribute album, *[The Melody Lingers On](/wiki/The_Melody_Lingers_On_(Etta_Jones_album) "The Melody Lingers On (Etta Jones album)"),* for the [HighNote](/wiki/HighNote_Records "HighNote Records") label.[[9]](#cite_note-Etta_Jones-9) Her last recording, a tribute to [Billie Holiday](/wiki/Billie_Holiday "Billie Holiday"), was released on the day of Jones's death.[[10]](#cite_note-10)

Jones married John Medlock, from Washington State. She died in [Mount Vernon](/wiki/Mount_Vernon "Mount Vernon"), New York, at the age of 72, from cancer.[[2]](#cite_note-Dead-2)[[7]](#cite_note-:1-7) Pearson arranged her funeral at one of their frequent performance venues, a church in Mount Vernon, New York. Various jazz musicians played tribute to Jones at the funeral.[[3]](#cite_note-:0-3)

Jones earned the Eubie Blake Jazz Award and a Lifetime Achievement Award from the International Women in Jazz Foundation.[[11]](#cite_note-11)
